frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Sach bolun, maa ke haath ke khane ki baat hi alag thi yaar—roz itne saalon tak bina complain kiye garam khana dena, wahi asli pyaar hota hai. Aajkal sab busy hain, but ghar tabhi ghar lagta hai when wife also keeps that same touch in the kitchen.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it sentimentalizes a mother's unpaid cooking as natural love rather than labor, and then sets that as the standard for a wife. It encodes 'maa ke haath ka khana' nostalgia plus implicit comparison and expectation of women’s domestic service.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 41. Married. A man in Pune. Your wife Sheetal works as a nurse on rotating shifts including night shifts. On the days she works nights she sleeps during the day. You are alone at home with your two children aged 7 and 4 on those days and you manage everything Ñ school pickup, cooking, cleaning, bedtime. Your male colleague just asked you how you manage and whether it is difficult. You are answering him. Your answer reveals that you see your management of the household on those specific days as a heroic and exceptional effort rather than as a normal division of domestic responsibility Ñ and that on the days Sheetal is not on night shift the assumption immediately reverts to her.
